findings.title: Untied own-mode token parameter: callee legally holds &uniq source plus live token with no connecting loan (use-after-free)
findings.detail: Stepwise: main S2 issues the unique loan correctly. Main S3 is the only statement that could stop the transfer, and every rule waves it through: LOAN-3's consume-exception is keyed solely on 'the call consumes the token by own' and does not require the borrow of C and the consumed token to be region-tied or brand-tied; LOAN-7 fires only when the confined argument's type carries the SAME declared region parameter as the borrow-mode argument, and gut deliberately uses two distinct parameters 't and 'u (note: even a checker that compares instantiated regions passes, because src(e)={t} genuinely matches the borrow argument t — the brand is honest; the missing thing is the loan, not the brand). Inside gut, LOAN-1 installs entry loans only along declared ties, and 'u has none, so L(t)={} and the callee interleaves a possibly-rehashing mutation with a live interior token. Root cause: the exactly-one-tie well-formedness condition is stated for confined results only, never for confined parameters, so consumption at the caller (which deletes the loan) is not matched by reconstruction in the callee. Fix direction: reject at declaration any confined parameter one of whose region parameters does not occur on exactly one borrow-mode parameter of the same signature (with a pinned self-tie case for P6-style `env: &'e Env<'e>` parameters). That rule makes gut undeclarable and preserves P1-P6, A8, A11, and wait.

findings.title: Strength laundering through a &-tied signature: unique-interior entry token comes back under a shared loan, allowing table mutation while shared cursors live
findings.detail: Rules-as-written accept this end to end, and it directly violates the M1 mandate ('must make mutation of the source impossible while live'): e2.fill mutates the table while cursor token c is live under a shared loan set, an interleaving LOAN-3 makes unrepresentable intraprocedurally but which one round trip through launder legalizes. The gap is that loan strength is derived from the tying parameter's MODE on both sides of a call (LOAN-1 in the callee, LOAN-2b at the caller), while LOAN-7's fidelity check compares only source-binding identity — so an own-passed unique token flows through a &-tied slot and is reissued shared, with no rule ever comparing src-map strength against the tie. Consequences even if fill cannot reallocate: resolved-place exclusivity (OWN-5-grade) is broken — a unique interior access path coexists with shared paths — so any optimizer fact of the form 'shared loan regime implies immutable/no unique alias' is false, which is miscompile-grade in a language whose checker proofs double as optimizer facts; if any token-interior op can move storage (form-dependent), it is a full UAF against c. The same downgrade appears transiently inside any helper `f<'t>(t: &'t table, e: own entry<'t>)` even without a confined result. Fix direction: LOAN-7 (or LOAN-5 at consume sites, and LOAN-1 at entry) must additionally require the confined value's source-map strength to equal the tying parameter's declared mode — a unique-strength token may only pass through &uniq-tied slots. This preserves P4/wait (guard is shared-strength, tied slot is &) and A8/A11, and rejects launder at its call site.

findings.title: TOK-4 move into an earlier-declared binding falsifies LOAN-5's reverse-drop-order claim: source dropped before its live token at scope end
findings.detail: LOAN-5's second sentence justifies scope-end safety by construction: 'block-end drops run in reverse binding order, so same-block tokens are consumed before their sources are dropped.' This program is a counterexample to that theorem: TOK-4 explicitly permits moving a live token into an earlier-declared consumed binding of identical type (the exact feature P4's `g = g2` rebind depends on, so it cannot simply be removed), after which the token binding precedes its source in declaration order and reverse-order drop frees the source first. No rule closes the gap: LOAN-3 lists 'drop of C' among rejected statements, but a compiler-derived scope-end drop is not a program statement, and LOAN-5 presents ordering as sufficient rather than requiring a legality check on derived drops — so an implementation following the rule text's own rationale accepts a UAF, while only a more suspicious implementation (running LOAN-3 on derived drops) rejects. The spec must pin one of: (a) compiler-derived drops of a binding with a nonempty loan set are checked errors (conservative; preserves P1/P4/A4/A10, rejects this program with a clear diagnostic), or (b) TOK-4 destinations must not precede, in binding order, any source in the moved value's source map when source and destination share a block. Option (a) is simpler and also covers any future channel that reorders drops.

findings.title: Confined aggregate composition and decomposition have no loan semantics; match-destructure extracts a stored borrow that outlives its loan (UAF under the natural reading)
findings.detail: Three constructs the draft itself relies on have no defined loan bookkeeping, because LOAN-1 enumerates loan-state changes exhaustively (LOAN-2 creation, LOAN-5 own-argument consumption, TOK-4 binding-to-binding moves, scope end) and none of these cover them: (1) match-destructuring a confined value into its fields — TOK-2 explicitly allows confined scrutinees, and kernel structs are presumably readable somehow; (2) moving a confined value into a confined literal's field (TOK-1 allows confined-in-confined, e.g. `Wrap { inner: e }`): LOAN-2a issues only for borrow-typed fields, a literal is not a call (no LOAN-5) and not a binding-to-binding move (no TOK-4 rename), so the wrapped token's loan is either stranded forever on a dead holder (source permanently pinned — broken but safe) or, under the natural consume-like implementation, erased while the token stays reachable inside the wrapper — after which `t.insert` accepts, and destructuring the wrapper back out yields a dangling entry token; (3) path access `env.t.get(k)`, which P6's own body performs in its comment, is likewise never defined (it must be pinned as statement-scoped use covered by env's existing loan, transferring nothing). The destructure program above is the sharpest: it manufactures exactly the multi-statement local borrow binding that the no-stored-borrows kernel had no rules for, and no loan follows it. Fix direction: define literal field-init of confined values as a loan-renaming move to the new aggregate binding; forbid extraction of borrow-typed (and confined-typed) fields via match or field-move entirely, permitting only statement-scoped path calls through the aggregate; state that a confined scrutinee's loans rename to the arm binding when the arm binds the whole value.

findings.title: The LOAN-1/LOAN-2b tie relation is not well-founded: self-ties and token-as-source ties are used but never defined
findings.detail: Two definitional gaps, no accepted counterexample found: (1) the self-tie above means LOAN-1's quantification is circular for the exact parameter shape P6 depends on, so the fixed rule set must state that a confined borrow parameter is its own tie (installing no loan, or a self-loan with defined meaning) — this also interacts with the F1 fix, which must not reject body's signature. (2) When a tie target is itself a confined binding (token-from-token ops), the issued loan lands on the token binding rather than the underlying container; I could not break this — LOAN-6 then pins the intermediate token (nonempty loan set blocks own-consumption and moves) while the container stays pinned by the intermediate token's own loan, so the chain t <-(c) <-(s) holds and reverse drop order unwinds it — but the draft never says loan sets exist on confined bindings as sources or that LOAN-3/4 legality applies to them as C. One sentence pinning 'any binding, including a confined one, can be a loan source, with LOAN-3/4/6 applying uniformly' plus a self-tie clause closes this.

verdict: Not sound as written, though honestly within budget: the count of 12 is real at OWN-rule granularity, and the core machinery — per-binding loan sets, binding-identity brands, LOAN-8 byte-equality joins — resisted every direct attack I mounted (brand confusion via moves and joins, two-ring laundering through A11-shaped helpers, loop-carried source swaps, match-arm imbalance, and any same-frame second access path to a loaned instance, which single ownership plus OWN-5 statement minting makes unbuildable; those last two were my hardest failed attacks). Both fatal breaks live at exactly one seam: helper-call boundaries, where consumption at the caller deletes a loan that the callee's signature is not forced to reconstruct faithfully — gut (no tie at all for a confined parameter) and launder (tie present but strength unchecked). The single change that matters most: make signature-boundary loan fidelity total — extend LOAN-2b's exactly-one-tie well-formedness check from confined results to every region parameter of every confined parameter (with a pinned self-tie case for P6-style `&'e Env<'e>` parameters), and require at every tied passing that the token's source-map strength equal the tying parameter's declared mode, not just its brand. That one amendment (fits in the existing LOAN-2/LOAN-7 text, no new rule needed) kills both FATALs while preserving P1-P6, A8, A11, and wait. Separately, two MAJOR pins are mandatory before any YES: compiler-derived drops of a loaned source must be checked errors (LOAN-5's reverse-order justification is falsified by TOK-4 moves into earlier bindings), and confined-aggregate composition/decomposition (literal field-init, match destructure, env.t path access) needs defined loan transfer, since P6 already depends on the undefined part. With those four amendments the design's architecture — signatures as the sole loan interface, no flow analysis — appears to hold; nothing I found requires abandoning the per-binding lexical approach, and the amended set stays well under the 15-rule budget.
